title: A spiritual geography of Sufism
subtitle: Nine lands, nine flavours of one same path
sources: Nile Green, Sufism: A Global History (Wiley-Blackwell, 2012)
J. Spencer Trimingham, The Sufi Orders in Islam (Oxford, 1971)
intro: Sufism did not develop in a single piece. With each land it crossed, it took on a particular colour — the juridical sobriety of Baghdad, the flamboyant metaphysics of Andalusia, the inner silence of Bukhara, the Persian poetry of Shiraz, the qawwâlî of Ajmer, the fraternity of West Africa. One same water, nine vessels.

regions.name: Iraq — the cradle
regions.intro: Everything begins here. The first ascetics, the first ecstatic utterances, the first spiritual vocabulary: Iraq is the cradle of the path. Baghdad — capital of the Abbasid caliphate — becomes in the 9th century the epicentre where Junayd codifies sober mysticism, where Hallâj pushes it to the extreme, where Ghazâlî will reconcile Sunni orthodoxy and inner experience.
regions.body: In the south, at Basra, Hasan al-Basrî sets the tone of asceticism, and Râbiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya invents pure love. Further south still, in the marshes of the Bataʾih, Ahmad ar-Rifâʿî founds a popular path with spectacular practices. In the north, at Mosul and Tikrit, live the first Christian recluses from whom the Muslim ascetics draw inspiration. From the 9th to the 12th century, the spiritual centre of the Islamic world is here.
regions.spirit: Sobriety, codification, the first spiritual language. It is here that Sufism takes name and form.

quote.text: The knowledge of oneness proper to the Sufis consists in extracting eternity from temporality.
quote.author: Junayd of Baghdad

regions.name: Khorasan & Central Asia
regions.intro: At the confines of Iran and Central Asia stretches Khorasan — a land of caravan oases, of learned cities and of visionary spirituals. It is here that the “path of blame” (malâma) is born, here the first great manuals of Persian Sufism, and later the path of perfect sobriety: the Naqshbandiyya.
regions.body: At Bistâm, Abû Yazîd knows his dazzling intoxication. At Nishapur, the malâmatiyya hides inwardness beneath the veil of anonymity. At Mayhana, Abû Saʿîd Abû l-Khayr founds one of the first organised Sufi communities. At Herat, ʿAbdullâh Ansârî transmits a Persian mystical language of unequalled delicacy. Further east, at Bukhara and Samarkand, the lineage of the Khwâjagân — Ghijduwânî, Hamadânî — gives birth to the Naqshbandiyya, which will radiate throughout Islam.
regions.spirit: Inner silence, sobriety, malâma (the path of blame), a spirituality of the oasis and the caravan.

quote.text: The heart must be with God, the foot at work.
quote.author: Bahâʾ al-Dîn Naqshband

regions.name: Andalusia & the Maghreb
regions.intro: In the West of Islam, between the Iberian peninsula and the Sahara, there unfolds a Sufi tradition of striking originality. It is here that Ibn ʿArabî is born, the “Great Master” of mystical metaphysics. It is here that Abû Madyan transmits the heritage of Baghdad towards the Maghreb. It is here that al-Shâdhilî founds the path of present sobriety.
regions.body: Cordoba, Seville, Murcia: medieval Andalusia is an intense intellectual hearth where mysticism rubs shoulders with philosophy, where Jews, Christians and Muslims read one another. Fez, Marrakech, Tlemcen: the Maghreb becomes in the 13th century a new Sufi centre. In the 20th century, at Mostaganem, Ahmad al-ʿAlawî inaugurates the modern Western Sufi renaissance — it is through him that René Guénon, Schuon and Martin Lings will encounter Sufism.
regions.spirit: Flamboyant metaphysics (Ibn ʿArabî), the sobriety of presence (Shâdhilî), the modern Western renaissance.

quote.text: The real is the Real, the creaturely is the creaturely. The world is at once He and not-He.
quote.author: Ibn ʿArabî

regions.name: Egypt & Syria
regions.intro: A crossroads of the Islamic world, Mamluk Egypt and Ayyubid Syria welcome the paths come from the Maghreb and from Iraq, and give them a new impetus. Cairo and Damascus become spiritual capitals, lands of refuge and of diffusion.
regions.body: In Cairo, Ibn ʿAtâʾ Allâh al-Iskandarî codifies the Châdhiliyya in his Hikam. Ibn al-Fâriḍ, “prince of lovers”, sings there the mystical wine. At Tanta, the tomb of Ahmad al-Badawî becomes the greatest Sufi sanctuary of the country. At Damascus, Ibn ʿArabî dies in 1240 — his tomb is still visited there. In the 18th century, Cairo knows a major Sufi renaissance around Mustafa al-Bakrî and the Khalwatiyya, which spreads as far as Sudan.
regions.spirit: A crossroads, diffusion, doctrinal codification, popular sanctuaries.

quote.text: Your desires have kept you a prisoner; your renunciation of your desires would set you free.
quote.author: Ibn ʿAtâʾ Allâh al-Iskandarî, Hikam

regions.name: Anatolia & the Balkans
regions.intro: When the Mongols ravage Iran in the 13th century, the Persian spirituals flee westward. Konya, the Seljuk capital, becomes their refuge. It is there that the family of Rûmî settles, and there that the Mevleviyya is born — the order of the whirling dervishes. Ottoman Anatolia will then be, for six centuries, the land most densely populated with tekkes and orders in the Islamic world.
regions.body: At Konya, the circular dance (samâʿ) becomes prayer in movement. At Hacıbektaş, the Bektâshi order develops a humanist, mystical and Alevi reading of Islam. In Istanbul, under the Ottomans, dozens of orders coexist: Khalwati, Jerrahi, Naqshbandi, Mevlevi, Rifâʿi. Abolished by Atatürk in 1925, Turkish Sufism is reborn today, both in Turkey and among the diasporas of Europe and America.
regions.spirit: Intoxicated love, samâʿ, Bektâshi humanism, the Ottoman density of the tekkes.

quote.text: Our convent is not a place of despair: come, whoever you are, come.
quote.author: Rûmî

regions.name: Iran
regions.intro: Iran is the land where Sufism becomes poetry. At Shiraz, Hâfez sings the divine intoxication beneath the veil of the allegory of wine. At Nishapur, ʿAttâr tells the journey of the birds towards the Simorgh. At Kerman, Shâh Niʿmatullâh founds a path that still waters contemporary Iran. The Persian language becomes the vehicle of Sufism as far as the confines of Asia.
regions.body: Under the Safavids (16th century), Iran tips into official Shīʿism: the Sunni Sufi orders are persecuted, but Shīʿī Sufism (ʿirfân) develops, integrating the metaphysics of Ibn ʿArabî with the Imami gnosis. Mullâ Sadrâ will be its great thinker. Today, despite political vicissitudes, Sufism remains deeply alive in Iran — Niʿmatullâhî, Dhahabiyya, Khâksâriyya — and continues to nourish the poetic culture of an entire people.
regions.spirit: Persian poetry, mystical intoxication, Shīʿī ʿirfân, illuminative theosophy.

quote.text: The language of the birds is a language of love; without love, who would understand what they say?
quote.author: Farîd al-Dîn ʿAttâr

regions.name: India & the Indo-Persian world
regions.intro: India has welcomed Sufism since the 13th century, when Muʿîn al-Dîn Chishtî settles at Ajmer. In contact with Hinduism, with bhakti, with the yogis and the Indo-Persian culture, Indian Sufism develops a unique colour — closeness to the poor, the rejection of power, mystical music, openness to other traditions.
regions.body: At Ajmer, the sanctuary of Muʿîn al-Dîn becomes the greatest Sufi pilgrimage of the subcontinent — Hindus and Muslims go there together. At Delhi, Nizâm al-Dîn Awliyâʾ teaches universal fraternity. The poet Amîr Khusrau creates the qawwâlî as it is heard today. In the 17th century, the Mughal prince Dara Shikoh translates the Upanishads into Persian, writes The Confluence of the Two Oceans to show the convergence of Sufism and Vedānta. The reformist Naqshbandiyya (Sirhindî) will come to oppose this openness. In Sindh and the Punjab, the mystical poets Bulleh Shah, Sultan Bahu, Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai sing in the vernacular languages.
regions.spirit: Service of the poor, qawwâlî, interreligious dialogue, vernacular poetry, reformed metaphysics.

quote.text: Give to the heart the generosity of the river, to the soul the warmth of the sun, and to the body the humility of the earth.
quote.author: Muʿîn al-Dîn Chishtî

regions.name: Sub-Saharan Africa
regions.intro: From Senegal to Sudan, from Mauritania to Nigeria, African Sufism is today one of the most living spiritual forces of the Muslim world. It carried the deep Islamisation of the continent, structured whole societies, resisted colonisation, and continues to nourish the daily life of tens of millions of people.
regions.body: The Qâdiriyya, transmitted by the Kuntâ of Mauritania, and the Tijâniyya, founded at the end of the 18th century, are the two great paths of the Sahel. In Senegal, Ahmadou Bamba founds the Murîdiyya at the end of the 19th century: his tomb at Touba has become the centre of a unique spiritual and economic city. In the 20th century, Ibrâhîm Niasse spreads the “Fayda Tijâniyya”, communicating the mystical experience to hundreds of thousands of disciples. In Nigeria, Usman dan Fodio and Nana Asma'u — an exceptional learned woman — articulate Sufism, knowledge and social reform.
regions.spirit: A strong community, social structuring, anti-colonial resistance, oral and lettered transmission, the contemporary fayda.

quote.text: Knowledge is light; and light has no country.
quote.author: Amadou Hampâté Bâ

regions.name: The contemporary West
regions.intro: Since the 20th century, Sufism has taken root in Europe and America in plural forms. It comes at once through Muslim immigration, through the translation of the great texts, through the work of the perennialists, and through masters come from Turkey, Morocco, Iran, Senegal, India.
regions.body: Inayat Khan introduces a universalist Sufism in the United States as early as 1910. René Guénon (d. 1951), Frithjof Schuon, Titus Burckhardt, Martin Lings, Seyyed Hossein Nasr inscribe Sufism within perennialist thought. Eva de Vitray-Meyerovitch translates Rûmî into French, Henry Corbin reveals to the world the Iranian gnosis. Today, several paths are established: ʿAlâwiyya, Bûtchîchiyya, Naqshbandi-Haqqani, Mevlevi, Halveti-Jerrahi. Sufism also touches a non-Muslim public seeking a living spiritual path, through love, poetry, silence — Rûmî is today the most-read poet in the United States.
regions.spirit: Perennialism, translation, inter-spiritual dialogue, a universalist reading, diasporic transmission.

quote.text: Sufism appears as a universal wisdom, a living spiritual path, in Muslim lands as in the West.
quote.author: Éric Geoffroy
